notes: In his dream Jacob saw a ladder reaching from earth to heaven, with angels ascending and descending on it. Above the ladder stood the Lord, who (and we must assume that Jacob ascended the ladder) covenanted with Jacob, as he had with Abraham and Isaac, to bless him and his posterity throughout all generations. He was told, as had been his fathers, that through his seed all the families of the earth would be blessed. Joseph Smith tells us that the "three principal rounds of Jacob's ladder" were the same ascended by Paul (2 Corinthians 12:2) and that they represented progression from telestial to terrestrial, and from terrestrial to celestial degrees of glory (Smith, Joseph. History of the Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints. Salt Lake City: Deseret Book Co., 1927 (published by the Church). 5:402). (Joseph Fielding Mc Conkie, Gospel Symbolism [Salt Lake City: Bookcraft, 1999], 123.)

third heaven refers to entering the celestial realm. There are some commentators who point to the idea of seven heavens, but the "three-level cosomology is almost certainly older that the more eleborate seven-level version. The two models appear to correspond to the hierarchial structure of the temple" as it existed in Solomon's temple (three levels) and Herod's temple (seven levels). (C.R.A. Morray-Jones, "Paradise Revisited (2 Corinthians 12:1-12): The Jewish Mystical Background of Paul's Apostolate. Part 1: The Jewish Sources," Harvard Theoolgical Review, 86:2, April 1993, 205).
Paul had seen the third heavens, and I more. (Joseph Smith, Discourse of 17 May 1843; recorded by William Clayton; WJS, 202.)

Temple ordinances are to be kept sacred. The ordinances, rites, and ceremonies of the house of the Lord are sacred to faithful members of the Church, and to permit the gaze of the unholy would be to encourage the mockery of the scoffer and to invite the jeers of the enemies of righteousness. There are many instances when, for reasons best known to Himself, the Lord has revealed "his secret unto his servants the prophets" (Amos 3:7) with a command that they keep such revelation from the world. The Apostle Paul tells of one such who "was caught up to the third heaven and heard unspeakable words, which it is not lawful for a man to utter" (2 Corinthians 12:2-4). The brother of Jared was commanded to write the words of a revelation in a strange language and seal them up, and the Lord would show them in His own due time to the children of men (see Ether 3:27). The Apostle Paul spoke of a day "when God shall judge the secrets of men by Jesus Christ according to [the] gospel" (Romans 2:16). Members of the Church who have been admitted to the temple do not discuss even among themselves outside the temple these temple ceremonies because of their sacred character. (45-16, p. 137) (Harold B. Lee, The Teachings of Harold B. Lee, edited by Clyde J. Williams [Salt Lake City: Bookcraft, 1996], 575.)

There are some things in my own bosom that must remain there. If Paul could say, "I knew a man who ascended to the third heaven and saw things unlawful for man to utter," I more. (Joseph Smith, Discourse of 21 May 1843, recorded by Martha Jane Coray; WJS, 207.)
"paradise," a word that has various uses in scripture. In this case, Paul refers to the heaven where God dwells.

quotes.text: In this case, Paul refers to the heaven where God dwells. There are some things in my own bosom that must remain there. If Paul could say, "I knew a man who ascended to the third heaven and saw things unlawful for man to utter," I more.
quotes.attr: Joseph Smith, Discourse of 21 May 1843, recorded by Martha Jane Coray; WJS, 207.

notes: And if we want to give the members of the Church some understanding of the anguish the prophets feel, how about this tender insight from Moses, even after he has delegated some responsibilities: "I am not able to bear all this people alone, because it is too heavy for me" (Numbers 11:14). In preparing your students, as I think you must, to understand that life is not a bowl of cherries, that trials and tribulations will come to them, please distinguish between the kind of tribulations and trials which come to us because of our own stupidity and sin, those which come to us because of the structure of life in which the Lord maketh the rain to fall on the just and the unjust alike, and, third, those that I call the highest kind of tribulation, in which we know, as Paul did, the fellowship of Christ's sufferings. As people suffer that latter kind of tribulation, they are reassured that it is of the Lord, when the scriptures say, as did Paul, "There was given to me a thorn in the flesh" (2 Corinthians 12:7). "Given" is an action verb. Paul didn't say, "I happened to develop such a problem." Likewise, Mosiah 3:19 is the only place where sainthood is defined, and in addition to the attributes of love and patience, a saint is one who is "willing to submit to all things which the Lord seeth fit to inflict upon him." (Neal A. Maxwell, The Old Testament: Relevancy Within Antiquity, CES Old Testament Symposium, 16 August 1979)

7-10 Much has been written about Paul's "thorn in the flesh." Numerous hypotheses have been advanced by way of identifying the "thorn," including stuttering, epilepsy, a shrewish wife, an eye affliction, malaria, some mortal antagonist, or some spiritual weakness. Whatever it was, he knew why it was there, "lest I should be exalted above measure." He had pleaded with the Lord to take it away, but he learned, as we all do, that the Lord intentionally gives each of us weaknesses to keep us humble. Weaknesses are meant not to humiliate but to humble. If we let them accomplish their appointed purpose, weaknesses will not weaken us but strengthen us.

a thorn in the flesh The word (Gr. skolops) Paul uses here literally means 'a Pale' (as in impaled) or 'a stake.' It was used to refer to sharpened stakes, to surgical instruments, or to fishhooks. The very term suggests something that was extremely painful and troublesome to Paul. There have been endless debates on what such an infirmity might be, and the suggestions have included a bitter and shrewish wife who turned against Paul at his conversion, epilepsy, a serious eye affliction, malaria, and some spiritual weakness with which he was constantly troubled. There is no way of knowing from the present records what Paul meant.

Into every life there come the painful, despairing days of adversity and buffeting. There seems to be a full measure of anguish, sorrow, and often heartbreak for everyone, including those who earnestly seek to do right and be faithful. The apostle Paul referred to his own challenge: "And lest I should be exalted above measure . . . there was given to me a thorn in the flesh, the messenger of Satan to buffet me, lest I should be exalted above measure." (2 Corinthians 12:7.) (James E. Faust, To Reach Even unto You [Salt Lake City: Deseret Book Co., 1980], 98.)

quotes.text: Faust, To Reach Even unto You [Salt Lake City: Deseret Book Co., 1980], 98.) Some undergo searing developments that cut suddenly into mortality’s status quo. Some have trials to pass through, while still others have allotments they are to live with. Paul lived with his “thorn in the flesh” (2 Corinthians 12:7).
quotes.attr: Neal A. Maxwell, in Conference Report, Apr. 2000, 90; or Ensign, May 2000, 72

notes: "The number three is a reminder of Jesus' temptations in the Garden of Gethsemane and the three times he petitioned the Father to remove the cup from him." Martin There were also three temptations at the beginning of the Savoir's ministry.

After teaching 20 years, I was forced to retire. My chronic back pain had driven me to an early retirement. About the only place that was comfortable for me was on the carpeted floor. I had already had two back surgeries without much relief. My prayer to Heavenly Father was for a complete recovery. I forgot "thy will be done." In my painful anguish all I could envision was complete relief from my excruciating pain. I had been given blessings but I felt no respite to my satisfaction. In my desperation, I sat down and wrote a letter to each of my children and tried my best to explain why I had taken my life. I quietly tucked the letters in a box in the closet where surely they would be found after my demise. Enduring to the end is fine, I thought, if you do not experience daily chronic pain. Now all I had to do was think of a "clean" way to end my life. As I entered the shower, I began to sing. All of a sudden I realized I had not sung in weeks. I felt an exhilarating joy as the water hit my face. Later as I studied, I came upon this scripture. Never had a scripture meant so much to me at this time. My mode of praying changed from, "Please, Heavenly Father, heal me," to "Heavenly Father, grant me strength to bear the pain and not murmur." Am I pain-free? No, but I am able to sleep and endure the pain much better than before. My faith has increased greatly. Through my trials, I have learned that faith does indeed precede the miracle. Our prophets always encourage us to read, study and ponder the scriptures. I am eternally grateful for following that counsel. Failure to do so may have jeopardized my quest for eternal glory. (Dora E. Paulson, LDS Church News, March 15, 2003)

My grace is sufficient for thee: But grace is not infinite, and for the most part seems in the Divine economy limited to what is sufficient for the accomplishment of the task appointed to one instrument in a pattern of circumstances and other instruments. (J R R Tolkien Letter p326)

Our present adversities may have immediate value as well as preparing us for the future. Suffering is part of the divine plan and is essential to our exaltation. This life is "a preparatory state given to finite beings, a space wherein they may improve themselves for a higher state of being." (Discourses of Brigham Young, p. 345.) Those unwilling to withstand adversity will not be exalted. "Hatred and persecutions have been the lot of every man that ever lived on the earth holding the oracles of the Kingdom of Heaven to deliver to the children of men." (Brigham Young, JD 7:198) We must pass through the refining experience of sorrow as did Enoch, Noah, Melchizedek, Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob if we are to enjoy with them the blessings of the celestial kingdom. (Kenneth H. Beesley in Brent A. Barlow, ed., Understanding Death [Salt Lake City: Deseret Book Co., 1979], 30.)

quotes.text: This life is "a preparatory state given to finite beings, a space wherein they may improve themselves for a higher state of being." (Discourses of Brigham Young, p. 345.) Those unwilling to withstand adversity will not be exalted. "Hatred and persecutions have been the lot of every man that ever lived on the earth holding the oracles of the Kingdom of Heaven to deliver to the children of men."

Our present adversities may have immediate value as well as preparing us for the future. Suffering is part of the divine plan and is essential to our exaltation. This life is "a preparatory state given to finite beings, a space wherein they may improve themselves for a higher state of being."

notes: The Corinthians ought to have been the ones to oppose the false teachers and refute their accusations against Paul. Paul is hurt by their reception of his opponents. He regarded the Corinthians as the seal of his apostleship (1 Cor 9:2; 2 Cor 3:2,3).
Compare Romans 15:17-19. Paul can perform miracles as well as the next apostle (cf. Acts 13:11; 14:10; 15:12; 16:18; 19:11-12; 23:3-6,8) yet his claim to apostleship does not rest on these.

Paul is about to visit them for the third time (cf. 13:1). The first time was when the church was first founded under Paul's ministry, the second the 'painful' visit referred to by implication in 2:1.

be spent (ejkdapanaomai) spend oneself fully dapanaw may refer to spending resources (money Paul had earnt or had been sent from other churches) while ejkdapanhqhsomai refers to Paul's own energies being spent on the Corinthians - even being willing to sacrifice his life for them. "The point is that ... Paul will not withold any resource he has, including himself." Martin

Paul is responding to the charge that he and his companions have cheated the Corinthians - while outwardly refusing to receive support from them they have arranged the collection to line their own pockets.
In verses 19-21 Paul urges the Corinthians to repent before he comes.

This list includes issues which Paul had sought to address in 1 Corinthians. Verse 20 lists sins of a church rent by division. The following verse describes a church troubled by sexual immorality. These two weaknesses were evidently typical of Corinth.

"Paul is quite concerned that he will have to take decisive action against those of Corinth who persist in their sin. He will not shrink from such an unhappy task if it will help his children." Martin
It seems that Paul is referring to those who continued to sin and failed to repent despite his earlier visit or letter.
